title: Smoke on Upholstery
stainChemistry: Smoke residue (soot) contains carbon particles, tar, nicotine (alkaloid), and volatile organic compounds. On upholstery, carbon particles lodge mechanically in fiber texture while tar creates a sticky film. Nicotine produces yellow-brown staining through chemical bonding. The odor comes from VOCs absorbed into the fiber matrix. Fire smoke also contains acidic gases that can damage fibers.
whyThisWorks: Dry cleaning (professional) is most effective because solvent dissolves tar without driving soot deeper into upholstery. For DIY, surfactant with baking soda addresses the acidic soot. An oxidizer targets nicotine yellowing. Odor requires thorough airing or ozone treatment. Vacuuming loose soot BEFORE wet treatment prevents smearing.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Vacuum loose soot — do NOT brush or wipe (smears carbon into fiber)
2. Apply POG to dissolve tar component — 5-10 min dwell time
3. Apply NSD to remove remaining soot and nicotine — 5-10 min dwell time
4. If yellowing remains: apply H2O2 6% — 5-10 min dwell time
5. Flush with cool water. Air dry in ventilated area
warnings: Always test on hidden area first

steps: 1. Remove loose material — brush, blot, or scrape as appropriate
2. Apply Dawn dish soap mixed with cold water — 5-10 min dwell time
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat if needed
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional for specialized treatment
warnings: Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first
Air dry until stain is confirmed gone — heat sets most stains

neverDo: No over-wetting — causes water rings and backing damage
No chlorine bleach on colored fabric
Always check care code (W, S, WS, X) before treating
fiberSensitivities: Mixed fiber blends common — treat for the most sensitive fiber present
Cushion foam absorbs moisture — avoid saturation
Test in hidden area before treating visible sections

escalation.when: If smoke stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is smoke on upholstery, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
